exercises.title: Calculate error budget from SLO
exercises.instructions:
Razorpay SLO: 99.9% availability. Month has 43,200 minutes.
1. What is the error budget in minutes?
2. If 15 minutes of downtime occurred, what % of budget is consumed?
3. How many minutes remain?
exercises.expected_solution:
1. Error budget = (1 - 0.999) × 43,200 = 0.001 × 43,200 = 43.2 minutes

2. Budget consumed = 15 / 43.2 × 100 = 34.7% consumed

3. Budget remaining = 43.2 - 15 = 28.2 minutes

Context:
  If it's day 15 of 30 (50% through month):
    Budget consumed = 34.7% (< 50% through month) → HEALTHY (consuming slower than rate)
  If it's day 5 of 30 (16% through month):
    Budget consumed = 34.7% (> 16% through month) → AT RISK (consuming faster than rate)
    Burn rate = 34.7% / 16% = 2.2× the allowed rate → slow burn alert may fire

test_cases.expected:
1. Error budget = (1 - 0.999) × 43,200 = 0.001 × 43,200 = 43.2 minutes

2. Budget consumed = 15 / 43.2 × 100 = 34.7% consumed

3. Budget remaining = 43.2 - 15 = 28.2 minutes

Context:
  If it's day 15 of 30 (50% through month):
    Budget consumed = 34.7% (< 50% through month) → HEALTHY (consuming slower than rate)
  If it's day 5 of 30 (16% through month):
    Budget consumed = 34.7% (> 16% through month) → AT RISK (consuming faster than rate)
    Burn rate = 34.7% / 16% = 2.2× the allowed rate → slow burn alert may fire

hints: Error budget = (1 - SLO) × total_minutes_in_month
Budget consumed% = actual_downtime / budget × 100
Context matters: same downtime is healthy mid-month, critical early-month

exercises.title: Complete the burn rate alert PromQL
exercises.instructions: Fill in: fast burn alert. SLO = 99.9% (error_budget = 0.001). Fast burn multiplier = 14.4× (1h window).
exercises.starter_code:
# Fast burn alert — fires if consuming 5% of monthly budget in 1 hour:
___(
  ___(payments_total_total{status='failure'}[1h])
) /
___(
  ___(payments_total_total[1h])
) > _____ * 0.001
exercises.expected_solution:
# Fast burn alert — fires if consuming 5% of monthly budget in 1 hour:
sum(
  rate(payments_total_total{status='failure'}[1h])
) /
sum(
  rate(payments_total_total[1h])
) > 14.4 * 0.001
hints: sum() to aggregate across multiple instances
0.001 = 0.1% error budget for 99.9% SLO

exercises.title: Define SLIs for 3 Razorpay user journeys
exercises.instructions:
Write SLI definitions (as PromQL comments) for:
1. Payment success: % of payments that succeed
2. Payment latency: % of payments completing < 500ms
3. Checkout availability: % of checkout page loads that succeed
hints: SLI = good events / total events (always a ratio 0-1)
Histogram bucket le='2': counts requests completing in ≤ 2 seconds
User-centric: SLI measures what the user experiences

exercises.title: Write prometheus alert rules for payment service
exercises.instructions:
Write prometheus/rules.yml with 3 alert rules:
1. PaymentErrorHigh: error rate > 1% for 5 minutes (warning)
2. PaymentP99High: p99 > 500ms for 5 minutes (warning)
3. PaymentServiceDown: no metrics for 3 minutes (critical)
exercises.expected_solution:
# prometheus/rules.yml
groups:
  - name: razorpay_payment_alerts
    rules:

      # 1. High error rate:
      - alert: PaymentErrorHigh
        expr: |
          sum(rate(payments_total_total{status='failure'}[5m]))
          / sum(rate(payments_total_total[5m])) * 100 > 1
        for: 5m           # sustained for 5 minutes (not a fluke)
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: payments
        annotations:
          summary: 'Payment error rate > 1%'
          description: 'Error rate: {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}'
          runbook_url: 'https://wiki.razorpay.com/runbooks/payment-error-high'

      # 2. High P99 latency:
      - alert: PaymentP99High
        expr: payments_duration_seconds{quantile='0.99'} > 0.500
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: payments
        annotations:
          summary: 'Payment p99 latency > 500ms'
          description: 'Current p99: {{ $value | humanizeDuration }}'
          runbook_url: 'https://wiki.razorpay.com/runbooks/payment-latency-high'

      # 3. Dead man switch — service stopped sending metrics:
      - alert: PaymentServiceDown
        expr: absent(payments_total_total)
        for: 3m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          team: payments
        annotations:
          summary: 'Payment service metrics missing'
          description: 'No payment metrics received for 3+ minutes'
          runbook_url: 'https://wiki.razorpay.com/runbooks/payment-service-down'
hints: for: 5m: prevents flapping on brief spikes
absent(): fires when metric has NO data (service down, scrape failing)
runbook_url: link to actionable steps — reduces MTTR significantly

exercises.title: Multi-window burn rate alert rules
exercises.instructions:
Write fast-burn and slow-burn alert rules:
1. FastBurn: error rate > 14.4× budget (1h window) AND > 14.4× (5m window)
2. SlowBurn: error rate > 6× budget (6h window) AND > 6× (30m window)
3. SLO = 99.9% (budget = 0.001)
4. Explain: why two windows are required
exercises.expected_solution:
# prometheus/rules.yml — burn rate alerts for 99.9% SLO (budget=0.001)
groups:
  - name: razorpay_burn_rate_alerts
    rules:

      # FAST BURN: consuming 5% of monthly budget in 1 hour
      # Multiplier: 14.4× (consumes full budget in ~2 days if sustained)
      - alert: PaymentFastBurn
        expr: |
          (
            sum(rate(payments_total_total{status='failure'}[1h]))
            / sum(rate(payments_total_total[1h]))
            > 14.4 * 0.001
          )
          AND
          (
            sum(rate(payments_total_total{status='failure'}[5m]))
            / sum(rate(payments_total_total[5m]))
            > 14.4 * 0.001
          )
        for: 2m   # brief for: — fast burn needs rapid response
        labels:
          severity: critical
          team: payments
        annotations:
          summary: 'FAST BURN: payments consuming error budget 14× faster than SLO'
          description: 'Action required within 15 minutes. Current rate: {{ $value }}'
          runbook_url: 'https://wiki.razorpay.com/runbooks/fast-burn'

      # SLOW BURN: consuming 10% of monthly budget in 6 hours
      # Multiplier: 6× (consumes full budget in ~5 days if sustained)
      - alert: PaymentSlowBurn
        expr: |
          (
            sum(rate(payments_total_total{status='failure'}[6h]))
            / sum(rate(payments_total_total[6h]))
            > 6 * 0.001
          )
          AND
          (
            sum(rate(payments_total_total{status='failure'}[30m]))
            / sum(rate(payments_total_total[30m]))
            > 6 * 0.001
          )
        for: 15m
        labels:
            severity: warning
            team: payments
        annotations:
          summary: 'SLOW BURN: payments consuming error budget 6× faster than SLO'
          description: 'Investigate within 1 hour. Current rate: {{ $value }}'
          runbook_url: 'https://wiki.razorpay.com/runbooks/slow-burn'

# WHY TWO WINDOWS?
# Single 1h window:
#   5 bad minutes at start of hour → error_rate_1h = 5/60 = 8.3%
#   Alert fires. But: issue resolved 55 minutes ago!
# Two windows (1h + 5m):
#   1h: 5 bad minutes → 8.3% (high) — yes, budget burned
#   5m: issue resolved → 0% (low) — NOT happening now
#   AND fails → alert doesn't fire for resolved issue
#   Only fires when BOTH windows confirm issue is ongoing
hints: 14.4× = 5% of 30-day budget consumed in 1 hour
6× = 10% of 30-day budget consumed in 6 hours
AND: both windows must confirm — reduces false positives on resolved issues

exercises.title: Write an actionable runbook
exercises.instructions:
Write a runbook for PaymentP99High alert in markdown:
1. Alert context (what is firing, typical causes)
2. Immediate triage steps (< 5 minutes)
3. Mitigation options (with specific commands)
4. Escalation criteria
5. Post-incident actions
exercises.starter_code:
// Runbook: PaymentP99High
// Format: markdown (as a Java multi-line string for the exercise)
exercises.expected_solution:
public class PaymentP99HighRunbook {
    // Runbook stored in Git: https://wiki.razorpay.com/runbooks/payment-latency-high
    public static final String RUNBOOK = """
        # Runbook: PaymentP99High
        
        ## Alert Definition
        - **What**: p99 payment processing latency > 500ms for 5 minutes
        - **Severity**: Warning (page if also consuming fast burn)
        - **SLO Impact**: latency SLO target: 95% of payments < 2s
        
        ## Common Causes (check in this order)
        1. Visa/Mastercard gateway slow → check external status pages
        2. DB connection pool exhausted → check HikariCP metrics
        3. Recent deployment → check Grafana deployment annotations
        4. JVM GC pause → check GC pause rate metric
        5. Redis cache cold → check cache hit rate panel
        
        ## Immediate Triage (< 5 minutes)
        ```
        1. Open Grafana: payment-service dashboard
           - Is throughput normal? Low throughput + high p99 = different cause than high throughput
        2. Check circuit breaker state: resilience4j_circuitbreaker_state
           - If OPEN: fallback active, payments queued
        3. Check Zipkin: find a slow trace
           - Which span is slow? visa.charge? db.query?
        4. Check logs: {app='payment-service', level='ERROR'} | json | durationMs > 1000
        ```
        
        ## Mitigation Options
        - Gateway slow: increase circuit breaker timeout, enable fallback
          `kubectl set env deployment/payment-service VISA_TIMEOUT_MS=5000`
        - DB pool full: scale up DB or increase pool size (short-term)
          `kubectl set env deployment/payment-service HIKARI_MAX_POOL=100`
        - Recent deploy: roll back
          `kubectl rollout undo deployment/payment-service`
        
        ## Escalate If
        - p99 > 2 seconds (SLO breach imminent)
        - Error budget < 20% remaining (freeze deployments)
        - Multiple services affected simultaneously
        
        ## Post-Incident
        1. Write postmortem within 48 hours
        2. Add monitoring for identified root cause
        3. Update this runbook with new findings
        """;
}
hints: Runbook = first thing on-call reads during incident
Common causes ordered by frequency: most common first
Specific commands: kubectl/curl commands ready to copy-paste

exercises.title: Error budget dashboard implementation
exercises.instructions:
Write error budget tracking in Java:
1. ErrorBudgetTracker @Service: tracks budget consumed vs allowed
2. Uses Counter values to compute SLI
3. Gauges: budget_remaining_percent, budget_consumed_minutes
4. Alerts when < 20% remaining
5. @SpringBootTest: 2 tests verifying budget calculations
hints: budgetRemainingPercent = (1 - errorRate/budget) × 100
shouldFreezeDeployments: < 20% remaining = freeze feature work
Test reset: need to clear counters between tests

exercises.title: Alert on symptoms not causes
exercises.instructions:
Evaluate these 6 alerts — which are symptom-based (good) vs cause-based (bad)? Rewrite the cause-based ones:
1. CPU > 80%
2. Payment error rate > 1%
3. Thread pool queue depth > 100
4. p99 latency > 500ms
5. DB connection pool > 90%
6. JVM heap > 80%
hints: Symptom: user experiences this directly (slow, failing)
Cause: internal metric that might lead to symptoms (high CPU, full pool)
Correlating cause+symptom is OK: pool > 90% AND error rate > 0.5%

exercises.title: Dead man switch implementation
exercises.instructions:
Write a 'dead man switch' — an alert that fires when metrics STOP arriving:
1. absent() PromQL to detect missing metric
2. @Scheduled heartbeat that keeps the metric alive
3. If app crashes: heartbeat stops → metric absent → alert fires
4. @SpringBootTest: heartbeat increments counter
hints: absent(): fires when the metric has NO data points in Prometheus
for: 3m: wait for a few scrape cycles before alerting on absence
This is the most important alert — all others fail silently if service is dead

exercises.title: Which alert fires for which scenario?
exercises.instructions:
Four alerts configured:
  A. PaymentErrorHigh: error% > 1% for 5m (warning)
  B. PaymentFastBurn: 14.4× burn rate on 1h+5m windows
  C. PaymentP99High: p99 > 500ms for 5m
  D. PaymentServiceDown: absent(metric) for 3m

For each scenario, which alert(s) fire?
1. Visa gateway down at 7pm — all payments fail, 100% error rate
2. DB connection pool at 95% — payments slow (p99=800ms) but not failing
3. Payment service process killed (crash)
4. High traffic burst — p99 spikes to 600ms for 90 seconds then recovers
exercises.expected_solution:
Scenario 1: Visa gateway down, 100% error rate
  → A. PaymentErrorHigh: FIRES (error% = 100% >> 1%)
  → B. PaymentFastBurn: FIRES (error rate >> 14.4× budget; both 1h and 5m high)
  → C. PaymentP99High: likely FIRES (failing calls still have high latency before timeout)
  → D. PaymentServiceDown: does NOT fire (service is up, just failing)
  → Action: page on-call immediately (B is critical)

Scenario 2: DB pool 95%, p99=800ms, no failures
  → A. PaymentErrorHigh: does NOT fire (error% = 0%)
  → B. PaymentFastBurn: does NOT fire (no errors = no budget burn)
  → C. PaymentP99High: FIRES after 5 minutes (p99=800ms > 500ms for 5m)
  → D. PaymentServiceDown: does NOT fire
  → Action: Slack warning, investigate DB pool — not critical yet

Scenario 3: Service process crashed
  → A, B, C: do NOT fire (no metrics = no data for PromQL to evaluate)
  → D. PaymentServiceDown: FIRES after 3 minutes (absent() detects missing metric)
  → This is why D is the most important alert!

Scenario 4: 90-second p99 spike to 600ms then recovers
  → A. PaymentErrorHigh: does NOT fire (no errors)
  → B. PaymentFastBurn: does NOT fire (no errors)
  → C. PaymentP99High: does NOT fire (for: 5m means spike must last 5 minutes)
  → D. PaymentServiceDown: does NOT fire
  → Result: no alert! for: 5m prevented a false positive on a brief spike.

test_cases.expected:
Scenario 1: Visa gateway down, 100% error rate
  → A. PaymentErrorHigh: FIRES (error% = 100% >> 1%)
  → B. PaymentFastBurn: FIRES (error rate >> 14.4× budget; both 1h and 5m high)
  → C. PaymentP99High: likely FIRES (failing calls still have high latency before timeout)
  → D. PaymentServiceDown: does NOT fire (service is up, just failing)
  → Action: page on-call immediately (B is critical)

Scenario 2: DB pool 95%, p99=800ms, no failures
  → A. PaymentErrorHigh: does NOT fire (error% = 0%)
  → B. PaymentFastBurn: does NOT fire (no errors = no budget burn)
  → C. PaymentP99High: FIRES after 5 minutes (p99=800ms > 500ms for 5m)
  → D. PaymentServiceDown: does NOT fire
  → Action: Slack warning, investigate DB pool — not critical yet

Scenario 3: Service process crashed
  → A, B, C: do NOT fire (no metrics = no data for PromQL to evaluate)
  → D. PaymentServiceDown: FIRES after 3 minutes (absent() detects missing metric)
  → This is why D is the most important alert!

Scenario 4: 90-second p99 spike to 600ms then recovers
  → A. PaymentErrorHigh: does NOT fire (no errors)
  → B. PaymentFastBurn: does NOT fire (no errors)
  → C. PaymentP99High: does NOT fire (for: 5m means spike must last 5 minutes)
  → D. PaymentServiceDown: does NOT fire
  → Result: no alert! for: 5m prevented a false positive on a brief spike.

hints: Absent(): fires when NO data; other alerts need data to evaluate
for: 5m: duration filter prevents alerting on momentary spikes
Crash: most dangerous — only dead man switch catches it

exercises.title: Complete SLO tracking service with error budget
exercises.instructions:
Build SLOTrackingService:
1. SLO = 99.9%, error budget = 0.1%
2. Gauge: slo.error.budget.remaining.percent
3. Gauge: slo.error.budget.consumed.minutes
4. Method: isBudgetHealthy() → false when < 20% remaining
5. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): full budget, exhausted budget, health check
hints: budgetRemaining = (1 - errorRate/BUDGET) × 100
0.1% errors with 0.1% budget = fully consumed (0% remaining)
0.01% errors with 0.1% budget = 10% consumed (90% remaining)

exercises.title: Alertmanager inhibition rules
exercises.instructions:
Write alertmanager inhibition rules:
1. If PaymentServiceDown fires: suppress PaymentErrorHigh and PaymentP99High
2. If InfrastructureCritical fires: suppress all warning-severity alerts
3. Explain: why inhibition reduces noise during major incidents
exercises.expected_solution:
# alertmanager.yml — inhibition rules

inhibit_rules:
  # If service is DOWN: suppress symptom alerts (error rate, p99)
  # Reason: service is completely down, error/latency alerts are obvious consequences
  # On-call should focus on bringing service up, not individual symptom alerts
  - source_match:
      alertname: PaymentServiceDown    # if this is firing...
    target_match_re:
      alertname: 'Payment(Error|P99).*'  # ...suppress these
    equal: ['team']                     # only if same team

  # If infrastructure is critical: suppress warnings
  # Reason: during a datacenter/k8s incident, individual service warnings are noise
  - source_match:
      severity: critical
      team: infrastructure
    target_match:
      severity: warning                 # suppress all warnings
    equal: ['datacenter']              # within same datacenter

# WHY INHIBITION MATTERS:
# Without inhibition during a crash:
#   1. PaymentServiceDown fires
#   2. PaymentErrorHigh fires (of course — service is down!)
#   3. PaymentP99High fires (of course — every request fails)
#   4. OrderServiceHighLatency fires (depends on payments)
#   5. UserAuthServiceError fires (depends on payments)
#   On-call: 15 PagerDuty pages at 3am, all for the same root cause
#
# With inhibition:
#   1. PaymentServiceDown fires → pages on-call
#   2. PaymentErrorHigh, PaymentP99High → SUPPRESSED (obvious consequences)
#   3. Downstream alerts → SUPPRESSED if marked dependent
#   On-call: 1 page. Fix the service. All suppressed alerts auto-resolve.

public class InhibitionDoc { public static void main(String[] a) {
    System.out.println("Inhibition: suppress obvious consequences of a known root cause");} }
hints: source_match: the alert causing inhibition (root cause)
target_match: alerts to suppress (obvious symptoms)
equal: only inhibit within the same team/datacenter/cluster

exercises.title: Zerodha complete SLO monitoring implementation
exercises.instructions:
Build Zerodha order service SLO monitoring:
1. SLO: 99.95% order submission success
2. SLOMonitor @Service: tracks success/failure, computes SLI, error budget
3. Alert rules: fast burn (14.4×, 1h+5m) and slow burn (6×, 6h+30m)
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): SLI computation, budget calculation, freeze check
5. All 3 pass
hints: SLO=0.9995: 99.95% availability
BUDGET=0.0005: 0.05% error budget
sli = ok/(ok+fail); budgetRemaining = (1 - (1-sli)/BUDGET) × 100

exercises.title: Production alerting anti-patterns — fix them all
exercises.instructions:
Each of these 5 alerts is problematic. Identify the anti-pattern and write the corrected alert:
1. Alert on every exception log line (no for: duration)
2. Alert when CPU > 50%
3. Alert with no runbook_url
4. Alert firing 24/7 including at 2am for a warning
5. Alert with threshold > 0 (any single error fires)
hints: for: 5m: most important fix for noisy alerts
Runbook URL: non-negotiable — reduces MTTR by 50%
Rate not count: avoid alerting on every single event

exercises.title: Complete M20 observability system — final synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build the complete Razorpay observability system for Module 20:
1. Metrics: Counter + Timer + Gauge (T20.1)
2. Trace: custom span (T20.2)
3. Structured logs: MDC + INFO/ERROR (T20.3)
4. SLO tracking: error budget gauge (T20.4 + T20.5)
5. Heartbeat: dead man switch (T20.5)
6. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): all subsystems verified

Module 20 complete synthesis — all tests pass.
hints: All 5 topics combined: metrics + tracing + logs + SLO + heartbeat
MDC.remove (not clear) in finally: preserve other MDC fields from filter
budgetRemaining gauge: Micrometer calls the function lazily on scrape
